coredata.dc:description: Quantum dot nanomaterials possess distinct optical and electrical properties from quantum confinement effects. These materials have notable benefits in diverse fields like solar energy conversion, bioimaging, and optoelectronic devices, owing to their high quantum yield and light emission that depends on their size. Nevertheless, the broad implementation of these technologies is impeded by problems such as exorbitant production costs, probable toxicity, and environmental instability. Novel synthesis strategies, such as nonorganometallic approaches and microwave-based procedures, are addressing these problems by strengthening safety, reducing expenses, and improving the photostability of quantum dots. This paper examines how quantum dots are created, methodologies for analyzing their properties, and possible uses in nanotechnology. It emphasizes how quantum dots have the ability to transform nanotechnology and overcome existing technological constraints. This review focuses on the most recent developments in quantum dot synthesis, exploring their diverse applications across multiple disciplines and discussing the ongoing endeavors to address the issues that come with them.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Quantum dot nanomaterials possess distinct optical and electrical properties from quantum confinement effects. These materials have notable benefits in diverse fields like solar energy conversion, bioimaging, and optoelectronic devices, owing to their high quantum yield and light emission that depends on their size. Nevertheless, the broad implementation of these technologies is impeded by problems such as exorbitant production costs, probable toxicity, and environmental instability. Novel synthesis strategies, such as nonorganometallic approaches and microwave-based procedures, are addressing these problems by strengthening safety, reducing expenses, and improving the photostability of quantum dots. This paper examines how quantum dots are created, methodologies for analyzing their properties, and possible uses in nanotechnology. It emphasizes how quantum dots have the ability to transform nanotechnology and overcome existing technological constraints. This review focuses on the most recent developments in quantum dot synthesis, exploring their diverse applications across multiple disciplines and discussing the ongoing endeavors to address the issues that come with them.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dot nanomaterials, which are tiny semiconductor particles, possess unique optical and electrical characteristics due to quantum confinement phenomena. [1–4]. These materials have a high quantum yield and size-dependent light emission, which makes them excellent choices for solar energy conversion, bioimaging, and optoelectronic devices [5–11]. Because of their unique size-dependent characteristics and quantum confinement effects, quantum dot nanomaterials are essential to advancing optoelectronic devices [12,13]. These zero-dimensional structures include photoluminescence and programmable bandgaps, making them useful for various uses, such as energy-efficient displays and biological imaging [1,14,15]. This study delves into the synthesis methods that customize the features of quantum dots for particular applications, such as chemical vapor deposition and colloidal synthesis [16–19]. To highlight the multidisciplinary nature of nanotechnology—which combines chemistry, physics, and engineering to promote advances in quantum computing and medicinal applications, it also looks at how material composition affects optical and electrical properties [20–27].

Organometallic techniques, which date back to the 1980s, emphasize bright, monodispersed nanoparticles but have drawbacks in terms of expense and toxicity [28–30]. By removing phosphorus and replacing dangerous precursors, nonorganometallic techniques aim to address these problems and increase the range of applications for QD [31,32]. Microwave-based techniques improve production rate and photostability, whereas microemulsion-based synthesis provides exact size control for core/shell QD creation [30,33]. For biomedical applications, direct aqueous-based synthesis using thiol-capping agents is a simple and less hazardous method [30,34,35]. Furthermore, despite difficulties obtaining precursors and sluggish synthesis processes, several alternative synthesis techniques, including thermolysis and metathesis, seek to produce group III–V QDs [36,37]. Together, these techniques add to the varied field of QD synthesis, meeting the needs of particular applications and resolving safety and environmental issues.

Variables such as reactant measurements, pH level, temperature, precursor concentration, and reaction time are manipulated during the production of quantum dots (QDs) to alter the properties of the nanostructure [30,38]. Though drawbacks include low resolution and time-consuming setup, characterization methods like transmission electron microscopy (TEM), small-angle X-ray dispersion, and UV-vis absorption spectroscopy may provide insights into QD characteristics [39–42]. Optical spectroscopy is used to help understand the electronic makeup and emission characteristics, whereas scanning tunneling microscope (STM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) are imaging methods used in structural research [43–48]. Associated illumination and electron microscopy research for uses such as molecular disease and toxicology are made possible by fluorescent persistence during electron microscopy sample preparation [49]. Together, these techniques add to the rich and varied field of QD synthesis [50–53].

These nanostructures, which differ from conventional bulk semiconductors in that they are just nanometers in size, have unique electrical and optical characteristics [54,55]. Because of their adaptable core-shell topologies and controllable photoluminescence, QDs are widely used in current technologies, including solar panels, LEDs, and photodetectors [56–60]. While QLEDs provide configurable emission wavelengths and better efficiency over standard LEDs, QD-based displays promise superior color accuracy and energy efficiency [61–67]. QDs provide affordable substitutes for conventional semiconductor technologies in photodetection, especially in the mid-infrared & ultraviolet regions [68–71]. Furthermore, QDs show great promise for biological imaging; they outperform traditional dyes in terms of luminescence, color contrast, and stability in vivo, and studies investigating possible toxicity issues are now underway [72–77].

Thanks to its benefits, such as high quantum effectiveness, color purity, and customizable emission wavelengths, quantum dots (QDs) are becoming an increasingly important component in next-generation displays [78–81]. In particular, micro-LED (μLED) displays and other QD-based light-emitting devices show great potential because of their improved performance parameters, including brightness, efficiency, and power consumption [82–85]. However, obstacles still stand in their general acceptance, particularly those related to environmental stability, nanotoxicity, and the wide output range of organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs) [86–88].

With their distinct qualities and functions, quantum dots (QDs) are nanomaterials with a wide range of uses in nanomedicine [89–91]. Because of their multifunctionality and potential for bio-functionalization, inorganic nanomaterials—including semiconductors like cadmium telluride (CdTe) and cadmium selenide (CdSe) are essential to biomedical research [92–94]. The objective is to enhance the properties of deep blue fluorophores, such as resistance to photobleaching, two-photon absorption cross-sections, and fluorescence quantum yields. This will be achieved by incorporating structural limitations based on the hexamethylazatriangulene motif [95].

QDs provide better optical properties than organic dyes [96–99]. However, problems like immunological reactions and toxicity continue to exist, which motivates further development into safer and more efficient QD-based technologies [100,101]. Hence, the imperative and pressing need lies in developing renewable energy sources such as hydroelectric, nuclear, solar, wind power, hydrogen, supercapacitors, and rechargeable batteries as storage devices. This approach provides a more sustainable and environmentally conscious solution to address the escalating energy requirements [102].

Incorporating quantum dots (QDs) into shows, solar cells, biomedical images, and sensors, among other fields, highlights their enormous potential and paves the way for further developments in nanomaterial research [103,104].

All branches of engineering and science are interested in nanomaterials and nanotechnologies, an actual multidisciplinary field encompassing physics, chemistry, materials science, and engineering. Nanomaterials are categorized into four categories based on their dimensions: zero, one, two, and three dimensions [105]. On the contrary, quantum nanostructures, particularly quantum dots (QDs), are categorized based on the degree of quantization. Additionally, the unique characteristics of zero-dimensional structures are explored, including size-tunable bandgap, multiple exactions production, and photoluminescence [106].

In Fig. 1 , it shows the basics of quantum dots. One may methodically investigate all of the essential facets of quantum dots, from their synthesis, characteristics, and applications, by dissecting the foundations of quantum dot nanomaterials into these three basic ideas. A synopsis of quantum confinement effects that highlight the role of quantum dot size in determining electron and hole behavior. The influence of quantum dots' size on their electrical and optical characteristics is discussed. Talk about how material selection impacts the bandgap and, in turn, quantum dot optical characteristics.

According to Cho, E., Jang, H., Lee, J., & Jang, E, the energy band gaps obtained from the theoretical band gaps were nearly identical to the experimental values and demonstrated a linear dependence on the inverse size of QDs [107]. Although the first ideas on quantum confinement in colloidal quantum dots (QDs) produced analytical band gap/size ratios or sizing functions, numerical techniques better describe size quantization. Nevertheless, researchers fit experimental band gap/size data sets using models with redundant, physically meaningless parameters that collapse upon extrapolation due to the lack of robust scaling methods [108]. At energies well above the band gap, as demonstrated by two methods in another experiment, the quantum dot volume scales with At. Its value is contrasted with an analytical computation utilizing the bulk semiconductor dielectric function and the MG practical medium theory. Both exhibit outstanding agreement [109].

There are many attributes to consider when talking about quantum confinement and size-dependent features, as Fig. 2 illustrates. "Quantum confinement" describes the three-dimensional spatial confinement of charge carriers, such as electrons and holes, inside a nanoscale semiconductor structure. Quantum confinement effects occur at the nanoscale when a semiconductor's size approaches that of the charge carriers' de Broglie wavelength. This confinement produces discrete electronic energy levels in quantum dots based on the dot's dimensions. In addition to causing a quantized band structure with distinct electrical and optical properties, confinement also causes the bandgap to widen with decreasing size. Because of quantum confinement phenomena, quantum dots display features that vary with size. Discrete electronic transitions result from quantizing energy levels caused by a quantum dot's size reduction. Larger bandgaps in smaller quantum dots cause their absorption and emission spectra to move to the blue side. The quantum dots' optical and electrical properties are also influenced by size, affecting carrier confinement, exciton binding energy, and density of states. Quantum dots can be tuned for various uses, including biological imaging, LEDs, displays, and control over their optical characteristics. In the study of semiconducting metal halide perovskite nanocrystals, the dark exciton photoluminescence (PL) and its fine structure are of great interest. However, there are contradictory reports regarding the level ordering of the bright and dark states and the accessibility of the emitting dark states [110–113].

One of the earliest nanotechnologies to be incorporated into the biological sciences for imaging or tracking macromolecules or cells in cells or tissue is quantum dots (QDs). The importance of QDs in biological and biomedical applications makes it crucial to discover a range of synthesis techniques for producing QDs with various properties [114]. Current semiconductor research offers multiple routes for synthesizing diverse materials to meet the needs of memory devices, optoelectronic and spintronic devices, energy applications, and human life [115].

Table 1 describes the synthesis techniques employed in quantum dots. These alternate fabrication methods provide researchers with more tools for synthesizing customized Quantum Dots, complementing conventional approaches such as chemical vapor deposition and colloidal synthesis. Depending on the application's needs, researchers can select the best method based on parameters, including control over quantum dot characteristics, scalability, and cost-effectiveness.
Before 2016, most research focused on lead chalcogenide QD-based solar cells; however, lead halide perovskite QDs have since emerged as rising stars [124].

Fig. 3 depicts the material composition used in quantum dots. Utilizing this understanding, scientists and engineers create quantum dots with surface, optical, and electrical properties tuned for various technological applications, such as quantum computing, biomedical imaging, lighting, and displays.

Semiconducting thin film materials are usually grown and controlled doped for modern electronic and optoelectronic devices. Using top-down processing of ordinary semiconductors, the ability to build nanoscale devices has been dramatically enhanced by developments in CVD, lithography, and molecular beam epitaxy [125]. They are investigating quantum dots' unique optical characteristics, such as their size-tunable emission and absorption spectra applications, based on their optical properties in biological imaging, lighting, and displays [126].

Quantum dot nanomaterials fundamentals explore the unique characteristics of quantum confinement phenomena, in which nanoscale semiconductor particles display discrete energy levels and controllable optical behaviors. Comprehending these fundamental concepts facilitates accurate manipulation of Quantum Dot production, composition, and applications, propelling advancements in quantum computing, biological imaging, and optoelectronics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis techniques for quantum dot nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: A quantum dot (QD) is a semiconductor crystal with dimensions in the nanometer range [127,128]. QDs can be created from metals (like Au), carbon-based materials (like graphene and carbon dots), or transistors (like Se, Cd, etc.). Quantum dots have exceptional optical features owing to their distinct size range and strong self-luminescence because of quantum processes [129]. When activated, smaller QDs produce more dynamic light [128]. In recent years, QDs have been widely used in biomedical research fields such as biological sensing, bioimaging, and disease identification. Because of their unique mechanical and physicochemical properties, QDs are suitable for bioimaging applications like fluorescent nanoprobes and protein chips [129]. Quantum dots (QDs) display a high surface-area-to-volume ratio because of their nanoscopic size and surface interactions with organic molecules [130]. Because of this feature, QDs are desirable drug carriers for biological purposes.

Quantum dots (QDs) are semiconductor nanostructures with unique electrical and visual characteristics that arise from the laws of quantum mechanics. Among these fantastic optoelectronic qualities are excellent quantum yields, excellent photostability, and a broad spectrum for absorption paired with narrow photoluminescent emission spectra. Compared to organic dyes, quantum dots (QDs) have a smaller emission band, which reduces interference in their detections. Furthermore, it has been demonstrated that QDs are far more stable than fluorescent organic dyes [131]. These appealing qualities have made QDs a handy analytical tool, creating a novel class of fluorescent probes with great potential for monitoring biological, environmental, and biomedical processes [132–134]. Quantum dot (QD) nanotechnology has profoundly affected several fields, such as chemistry, physics, and the biological sciences. These nanoparticles originate out of metallic or semiconductor materials, usually have a size range of 2 to 10 nm [135], include 102–104 atoms in their composition [136], and can be tuned for use in optics and electronics [137]. Furthermore, their shape can be precisely changed by adjusting the temperature, time, and ligand molecules used in their synthesis. Each step of the quantum dot synthesis process in nanomaterials is depicted in Fig. 4 .

The primary objective of synthetic methods is to produce monodispersed, highly bright nanoparticles with desired surface functionalities. Applying organometallic precursors, nanocrystalline particles were synthesized in the solution phase during the 1980s [138]. Subsequently, an applicable guideline was established for synthesizing QDs [139]. For the nucleation and growth of QDs with a limited size distribution, Murray et al. described a well-regulated approach in 1993 [140], while Talapin et al. and Peng et al. developed a hot injection-based method in the early 2000s [141]. Precursors, solvents, and surfactants—surfactant molecules may also function as solvents—are needed for the organometallic production of QDs [142]. A critical step in synthesizing QDs is selecting the proper precursors, as Steigerward et al. pointed out [143]. A reactive species required for nucleation stages and nanocrystal growth should be produced by the solvent rapidly breaking down precursor molecules, such as organometallic compounds, at the growth temperature. It has been possible to produce CdSe, TOPO, octadecene (ODE), and hexadecyl amine organic solvents with extended alkyl chains by using dimethyl cadmium (Cd (CH3)2) as a precursor for QD synthesis. Because of its high boiling point, cadmium (CH3)2 can make QDs at high temperatures.[144] Because they operate as a reaction medium for interactions between these hydrophobic organic compounds and unsaturated metal molecules, these chemicals play a crucial role in inhibiting the production of bulk semiconductors on the QD surface. Consequently, only nonpolar hydrophobic solvents like chloroform may make the organic ligand-capped nanocrystals soluble.

QD is synthesized in two steps: (i) nucleation, which is the process of precursors forming reactive monomers at high temperatures to generate nanocrystals; (ii) growth of the nuclei of the precursor units. Precise temperature control is necessary to separate the nucleation and development of nanocrystals. The mixture's temperature, the injection method, and the concentration gradient are generally the critical determinants of separating these stages. Photoluminescence (PL) is enhanced, and crystalline flaws can be eliminated more easily when high reaction temperatures, between 150 and 350°C, are used [145–149].
Surfactant molecules, including inorganic cores and nanocrystals, are also surrounded by an organic monolayer, which could result in novel materials [150].

Two domains—a nonpolar tail and a polar head group—are present in surfactants employed in synthesizing quantum dots. The nonpolar tail influences diffusion characteristics, whereas the polar head group usually affects the efficiency of binding [151]. The organic surfactants that control the solubility of the nanocrystals include compounds containing amine groups, phosphine oxides, alkyl phosphonic acids, alkyl phosphines, and fatty acids [152].

According to Yin et al.'s 2005 paper, adhesion energy creates a dynamic solvation that accounts for the adherence of the molecules of surfactant to the surfaces of the nanocrystals in the growth media and ought to be regarded as one of the essential elements in the growth phase [153]. This method allows for the mechanical characteristics of nanocrystals to be further chemically modified for use in other potential strategies [154] that attempt to synthesize conjugations between nanocrystals and biomolecules [155].

The reaction injection and temperature measurement typically happen through the flask's necks during synthesis, usually conducted in a three-neck flask attached to a Schlenk line. It is generally accepted to prepare nanocrystals in an inert atmosphere. The organic solvent and surfactant agents are added to the flask to synthesize QDs. During the solvation, the flask is heated to 150–300°C for 10–20 minutes while evacuating to remove pollutants [156]. Elemental chalcogens, such as tellurium (Te), selenium (Se), and sulfur (S), are introduced to the reaction media together with a capping agent. The elemental chalcogens have to dissolve in liquid trioctyl phosphine (TOP) or tributyl phosphine to produce a complex in this method [157–161]. Ostwald ripening is the process whereby material is redeployed onto larger particles in a colloidal system to facilitate their further growth when the extremely high surface energy of the smaller particles in the system stimulates their dissolution. Both the nucleation and growth processes significantly influence the dimension and form of the final particle. The synthesis of QDs is based on organometallic precursors, the most general method. The high reaction temperature (between 150 and 350°C) in this method enhances the PL features and aids in excluding crystalline flaws. Additionally, temperature or reaction time can quickly optimize the size distribution of QDs produced by this technique [162–164]. Surfactants in the growth stage of surfactant-aided synthesis of QDs are anchored to the surface of nanocrystals via an organometallic approach [165]. In addition to acting as surface-capping ligands, these compounds can stabilize nanoparticles, govern the solubility of nanocrystals, and regulate the development of QDs during synthesis to maintain the proper size [166]. Surfactant-based synthesis techniques, on the other hand, produce QDs with insulating organic ligands on their surfaces and can stop charge transfer between nanocrystals [167]. This limitation can be addressed by altering these organic ligands through the ligand exchange. This surfactant exchange on the surface of tiny crystals facilitates the development of QDs with various functions for particular applications, such as biomedical applications [168].

Transfer of charges between nanoparticles is required in optoelectronic devices. As a result, the perfect ligand should have features like high conductivity, stable colloids, and easy and stable electrical interactions [169]. It has been reported recently that CdSe QDs produced with ferrocene phosphine and derivatives of phosphine oxide as electroactive ligands have been created and characterized [170]. Based on this study, the phosphine-oxide functional group of the nanoparticles' inorganic cores directly connected them to ferrocene phosphine or phosphine oxide derivatives. The electroactive moiety has decreased due to lacking an alkyl chain spacer.
The distance between the particles and the electroactive moiety was reduced because these metallocene were directly attached to the nanoparticle's inorganic core.

In the meantime, the tertbutyl attachment, which served as the alkyl side chain, gave colloidal stability. This proved that these substances were suitable photoexcited hole conductors and photoredox devices.

However, several factors, including high reaction temperatures, high toxicity, high expense, and material instability, imply that organometallic techniques could be better for synthesizing quantum dots. While organometallic techniques show promise in producing narrower size distributions and better PL quantum yields, more environmentally benign techniques are the focus of research [171].

Organometallic-based synthesis of QD nanocrystals produces high-quality nanomaterials. However, because of their extreme toxicity and the fact that they require costly, pyrophoric ingredients, this approach still has significant drawbacks for various applications and potential explosive risk. For example, Cd (CH3)2, a highly poisonous compound sensitive to moisture and air, has been used as a precursor in this technique [172].

Alternative components include cadmium oxide (CdO), cadmium acetate (Cd (Ac)2), cadmium myristate, and others have been used to get around these restrictions. For instance, Reiss et al. have reported the fabrication of high-quality CdSe/ZnSe core/shell QDs.[173] and Li et al. by utilizing CdO instead of the conventional pyrophoric organometallic complexes [174]. Additionally, Qu et al. have revealed that CdO has been successfully included in synthesizing CdSe, CdS, and CdTe nanocrystals [175].

Additionally, while one-pot synthesis and certain organic solvents (such as amines and fatty acids) were used to create high-quality CdSe nanoparticles, typical high-quality CdSe nanocrystal fabrication was considered a strict approach. For instance, Flames et al. described a unique technique for the quick synthesis of nonorganometallic CdSe nanocrystals (80–85 %) that are monodispersed (3 nm with a size dispersion of 6.7 %), phosphine-free, and have a high yield. The foundation of this technique was a heterogeneous mixture of Se that was dissolved in ODE and then injected straight into a solvent (270°C) that contained CdO distributed in cadmium carboxylate [176–180]. Surprisingly, no protective environment was needed during synthesis since it did not impact the final product's quality. Furthermore, this technique has the potential to be scaled up for the synthesis of this kind of nanoparticle because the synthesis process may be carried out on an automated platform. Single-source precursors have recently been added to the high-temperature synthesizing II-VI nanocrystals.[181] However, more synthetic steps were needed to purify these precursors. Furthermore, the luminescence emission of nanocrystals generated from single-source precursors is often lower and occasionally exhibits polydispersed particles [182].

A hydrophilic agent is typically added to any immiscible solutions in microemulsion-based systems to stabilize them and generate a thermodynamically stable suspension [183]. Microemulsion procedures have become famous for conducting room-temperature QD development. They fall into one of two categories: reverse tiny emulsions (i.e., water in oil) or normal microemulsions (i.e., oil in water) [184]. It is well-established that a microemulsion approach is a valuable tool for regulating the creation of inorganic nanoparticles with restricted size distribution and ano dispersion. The procedure of QD synthesis in the tiny emulsion processes, where the freshly formed clusters are effectively stabilized and, as a result, the uncontrolled development of the QD crystals is blocked, is responsible for this high-quality formation [185].
Right now, the production of QDs involves the reverse micelle process, which creates a mixture of two immiscible liquids, such as polar water and nonpolar long-chain alkanes [186].

Many people accept the technique of adjusting the size and dispersion of particles. Furthermore, QDs with nuclei that match the morphologies of the QDs can be synthesized using this technique. Surfactants may create microscopic water droplets distributed in nonpolar liquids [187].

The pairs of hydrophobic and hydrophilic groups on opposite ends of the surfactant molecules generate tiny micelle droplets in solution using the converse micelle method. The solubility in the continuous oil phase results from the hydrophilic ends amassing in the center where the reactants are trapped. To speed up the rate at which exchange of the entrapped reactants—the outcomes of dynamic collisions—occur, the micelles are agitated vigorously [188]

Recently, ionizing liquid reverse micelles (IL RMs) were used to generate and stabilize CdS QDs in situ [189]. In summary, dioctyl sulfosuccinate prolinium isopropyl ester ([ProC3] AOT), an amino acid-based surface-active IL, was used to emulsify 2-hydroxyethyl ammonium format (HO-EAF) and isooctane to produce reverse micelles. Next, separate dissolves of Cd (NO3)2 ⋅ 6H2O and Na2S in HO-EAF were used to create CdS QDs embedded in microemulsions. This combination was then introduced to the reverse micelles, where it was agitated for a full day. Given its tunable light emission and lack of requirement for additional chemical treatment, this IL-QD hybrid system offered reasonable control over the size of nanoparticles (3.0, 3.4, and 5.7 nm in varied ratios, including 0.5, 0.7, and 1.0, respectively). It may also be employed in electroluminescent devices [190,191].

Additionally, II-VI core and core/shell QDs, including CdS, [192], ZnS/CdSe, [193], CdSe/ZnSe, [194], and ZnSe, have been produced via the reverse micelle approach [195]. Furthermore, new QD-based barcode systems for detecting cancer biomarkers have been created using the reverse micelle-based synthesis of QDs [196].

The amalgamation of micellar and sulfide-containing micellar solutions combined Zn2+, Cd2+, and Pb2+ encoding elements into a single QD [197]. This fabrication technique provides multiple benefits, including more control over QD size (which may be achieved by modifying the molar ratio of surfactant to water), a narrow size distribution, and simple QD dispersion. However, this technique has a few drawbacks, such as limited luminous yield and defect and impurity integration.

Microwave electromagnetic irradiation improves QD production rate, yield, and scalability. These benefits may stem from a phenomenon known as "dielectric heating," in which significant microwave absorption heats polar materials [198]. Dielectric heating uniformly heats the entire volume as opposed to convective heating.

As a result, there is less chance of thermal gradient effects, giving QDs' size and optical characteristics more control [199]. By creating a conventional thermal bath in which solvents or precursors can be heated directly, it is possible to control the rapid growth of nanocrystals by varying the polarity of the solvent and the precursor. Precursors, passivating ligands, solvents, and several microwave parameters like power, duration, and temperature all play a significant part in regulating the nucleation and growth processes and, consequently, the quality of produced QDs [200].

The temperature and microwave irradiation power used determines the optical characteristics. For instance, higher temperatures usually produce larger nanoparticles, as indicated by creating a red solution [201]. Chalcogenide precursors are among the critical substances utilized in microwave-based synthesis because they selectively absorb microwave radiation, allowing for the rapid nucleation and development of nanoparticles. As a result, reactant concentration and microwave power can be used to control and quickly grow QDs [202–204]

Rapid synthesis of water-soluble CdTe QDs (<5 m) with high fluorescence quantum yields of about 40–60 % has also been achieved using microwave irradiation [205,206]. Moreover, a novel approach to the production of CdSe(S) and ZnSe(S) QDs has been revealed [207]. 3-Mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) was used as a ligand by Qian et al. to lower the chemical reactivity of nanocrystal surfaces and act as a sulfide source in high-temperature processes. Moreover, the successful preparation of InGaP, InP, CdTe, and CdSe nanocrystals [208,209] and CdSe coated with ZnS [210] has been achieved through the use of a microwave heating technique.

It has been reported that water-dispersed CdSe/CdS/ZnS core/shell/shell QDs can be synthesized using a straightforward one-pot microwave irradiation method [211]. Next, 3-mercaptopropionic acid was used as a surfactant to disperse CdCl2. The following stage involved placing the produced Cd and Se precursors into a vessel and using microwave radiation to heat them to 100°C for 30 seconds [212]. Ultimately, the solution was cooled to 50°C using high-pressure air for two minutes to produce CdSe QDs with an emission wavelength of 572 nm. The CdSe core QDs had to be constructed first to make the CdSe/CdS core/shell QDs [213]. They were then added to a mixture of surfactant, Na2S, and Cd precursor. After that, the mixture was heated for five minutes to 100°C. In the end, CdSe/CdS/ZnS core/shell/shell QDs were produced by adding ZnCl2, N2S, MPA, and the produced CdSe/CdS QDs to a solution [214,215]. Subsequently, the mixture was microwave-heated for five minutes at 70 degrees Celsius. Using microwave irradiation, this approach produced QDs with a high PL quantum yield and improved photostability in 30 minutes, reducing the reaction time [216].

Regarding synthesis, aqueous-derived QDs are typically less expensive, simpler, and safer than nonaqueous-derived QDs, making them suitable for biomedical applications. As an alternate reductant for the water-based synthesis of QDs, the synthesis of water-based nanoscale crystals using thiol capping agents has gained popularity recently [217].

Two features distinguish this aqueous-based method: (i) thiol-ligands have a higher affinity than tri octyl phosphine oxide for conjugation with the surface of nanocrystals, and (ii) nucleation occurs at ambient temperature while crystal development occurs at a temperature of approximately 100°C [218]. Therefore, the controlled synthesis of magic-sized clusters may be more suited for direct aqueous-based QD manufacturing. Intermediates in the synthesis process, known as "magic-sized clusters," are made up of a particular number of atoms arranged in a way that makes their structures incredibly stable [219]. In 1996, Rogach et al. presented the aqueous-based production of CdTe nanoparticles capped with mercapto ethanol and thioglycerol [220]. Kapitonov and Rogach then reported the practical synthesis of thiol-stabilized CdTe QDs and CdSe QDs utilizing NaHX, where X is metal salt and an element such as Se, Te, etc [221]. Biothiols like cysteine, which have strong water solubility, biocompatibility, and a passivation effect, have also been utilized as stabilizers in manufacturing II-VI semiconductor nanocrystals [222].

The H2Te gas, created when concentrated sulfuric acid breaks down Al2Te3 powder chemically, is added to a solution of Cd (CO4)2 and thiol stabilizer by the traditional method for synthesizing CdTe. After creating no luminescent CdTe clusters through this procedure, non-luminescent solutions are heated under reflux at 100°C for a few hours to develop CdTe QDs [223]. The amount of time allotted for reflux can alter the size of nanocrystals because longer reflux times result in larger QDs. Higher concentrations of H2Te and a stabilizer and cadmium source are needed to create QDs larger than 6 nm [224].

In recent years, significant advancements have been made in creating and using group III–V QDs. However, the structure's enhanced covalently bonded components lead to low group III–V QDs crystallinity. Because of their weak crystallinity, group III–V QDs are not progressing as quickly as group II-VI QDs in synthesis techniques [225].

Furthermore, forming nanocrystals in most group III–V QDs took a lengthy period (up to several days), producing particles with a broad size distribution and identical absorption peaks. Furthermore, little progress has been made in the group III–V QD synthesis due to the intrinsic limitations of readily available precursors [226]. Thus, the lag in their synthesis procedures can make it challenging for group III–V QDs to produce and commercialize on a wide scale.
The following processes have been frequently considered for synthesizing group III–V QDs: transmetalation, 113, 116, dehalosilylation, metathesis, and thermolysis [227].

The metathesis reaction, sometimes called the double displacement reaction, is a chemical transformation in which the reactants exchange chemical bonds to produce products with bond types that are either similar or identical [228]. Group III–V bonds could be formed from alkali nitrides or arsenides as V-based precursors and from Ga- or In-halides as III-based precursors to construct group III–V QDs [229]. For reaction systems with metathesis or single source precursors, it usually takes several days or high temperatures (>400°C) to finish the reaction. Furthermore, the size of QDs synthesized using metathesis is challenging to control, leading to poly-dispersed nanocrystals [230].

Heat is the catalyst for the endothermic process known as thermolysis, which breaks down a single or two source materials. Pre-synthesized solid-state complexes containing both III and V precursors are typically thermally broken down to initiate processes for synthesizing group III–V QDs [231]. To meet the requirements of high temperature, reactions are also transported into a solvent with a high boiling point to obtain monodispersed group III–V nanocrystals [231].

Since its introduction by Wells et al., the dehalosilylation reaction—also known as tri methyl halo silane elimination—has been thoroughly studied by removing different ligands to produce group III–V QDs [232]. This allowed group-III halides and group-V organometallic precursors to be used in a coordinating solvent like TOP, TOPO, and quinoline. Examples of these precursors are tris(trimethylsilyl)arsine [As (TMSi)3] and tris(trimethylsilyl) phosphine [P(TMS)3] [233,234]. By controlling the sizes, dehalosilylation processes produced highly crystalline QDs at lower temperatures and faster timeframes [235].

Zinc blend bulk GaN at ambient temperature has an energy difference of 3.2–3.3 eV, making it a conventional group III–V QD with a broad and straight band. This property is significant for UV optoelectronics [236]. GaN semiconductors have the potential to be employed in current-injected laser diodes (LDs) and LEDs because of their superior thermal, chemical, optical, and radiation stability characteristics. The synthesis of solution-based GaN QDs was primarily concentrated on the metathetical reaction and thermolysis, owing to a deficiency of suitable N-precursors. The solvothermal metathetical process has been acknowledged as a viable wet-chemical method for manufacturing GaN QDs [237,238].

However, inadequate QD solubility may cause aggregation even with the low reaction temperature and satisfactory diameter control. Therefore, the synthesis of GaN QD has rarely used capping agents like TOPO [239]. In addition, precursor breakdown at a somewhat low temperature was used to initiate thermolysis processes, which were then used to synthesize GaN nanoparticles. This synthesis used single-source precursors with Ga and N already present in a single molecule [240]. Several amines, including N, N, N, N-tetramethyl-1, and 6-hexane diamine (TMHDA), were studied as surfactant/capping agents to control development and suppress agglomeration. By boiling TMHDA, gallium imide was pyrolyzed to produce blue-colored photoluminescent Ga-N QDs [241].

GaAs are Ga-based III-V semiconductors with a broad range of characteristics and can be applied to several diverse applications, including solar cells and contemporary optoelectronic devices [242]. Even without crystal flaws, homogeneous structures of GaAs can be created using the metal-organic chemical vapor deposition technique. However, this high-quality QD synthesis approach needs highly toxic precursors like arsine or pyrophoric compounds. Thus, much research lately has focused on developing a different wet-chemical synthesis approach that is less hazardous and more economical [243].

Carbon-based quantum dots (QDs) have garnered considerable interest since their initial identification. Supercritical fluids (SCFs), which possess distinct characteristics resembling gases and liquids, present a viable option for replacing current methods in commercializing and manufacturing superior-quality quantum dots (QDs). A thorough examination of current studies on producing carbon-based quantum dots (QDs) using supercritical fluid (SCF) technologies has been conducted. The study focuses on SCF-assisted exfoliation and continuous hydrothermal fluid synthesis methods. This paper describes the mechanics and features of carbon-based quantum dots (QDs) to understand how the types of precursors and synthesis pathways affect the characteristics of the QDs that are created [244].

Silicon quantum dots (QDs) are small particles of semiconductor silicon, typically ranging in size from 1 to 10 nanometers. These particles have significant potential for use in optoelectronic devices. Their biocompatibility, as compared to typical harmful Group II-VI and III-V metal-based QDs, enhances their practical use in preventing environmental contamination and damage to living creatures [245]. The properties of SiQDs have shown significant promise for several biological applications, such as their utilization in biosensors and bioimaging. GeQDs have garnered significant interest because of their unique electrical and optical characteristics associated with the quantum confinement effect, much like SiQDs. GeQDs have extensive applications in thermoelectric devices, enhancing electrical conductivity while reducing heat conductivity. Furthermore, the viability experiments demonstrate that GeQDs exhibit lower toxicity than conventional metal QDs [246]. Quantum dots with CdSe in the core and ZnS in the shell demonstrate a quantum yield of over 50 %. Applying shells to QDs enhances their quantum yield by deactivating nonradiative recombination sites. Also, it increases their durability under different processing circumstances, making them suitable for a wide range of applications. This approach has been extensively investigated as a means to modify the photophysical characteristics of quantum dots (QDs). The core and shell are usually made up of semiconductors belonging to types II-VI, IV-VI, and III-V, with specific compositions like (CdS) ZnS, (CdSe) ZnS, (CdSe) CdS, and (InAs) CdSe [247]. Various classifications of core-shell quantum dots (QDs) exist based on the relative positions of the valence and conduction bands and the crucial energy gap in semiconductors [248]. The band gap of the core is smaller than the band gap of the shell. Additionally, the band gap of the core is either inside the shell's band gap (type I) or vice versa (inverse type I). In the type II structure, either the core's valence band edge falls within the shell's band gap or the shell's conduction band edge falls within the core's band gap. Fig. 5 illustrates the alignment of band gaps for each of the four kinds.

Wells et al. developed a conventional wet-chemical production process for GaAs QDs using a deallocation reaction between gallium salt and [As (TMSi)3] [250]. When utilizing a dehalogenation procedure to create GaAs QDs, the solvent and precursors were crucial in determining the final product's shape, size distribution, and purity. It was reported that a reaction between GaCl3 and [As (NMe2)3] in 4-ethylpyridine as a coordinating solvent produced GaAs QDs with a limited size distribution [251]. Furthermore, a new and straightforward transmetalation reaction-based production of colloidal GaAs nanocrystals has been described [243]. One type of organic-metallic reaction is called a transmetalation reaction, in which organic groups or ligands are transferred from one metal to another. The fundamental idea behind synthesizing different organo-transition metal complexes is this reaction. Mechanistically, organic groups or neutral ligands are moved from primary group metals to transition metals during transmetalation [252]. It has been demonstrated that utilizing reagents with lower toxicity allows this wet chemical method to obtain a limited size distribution and highly crystalline nanomaterials.

The elemental gallium was created by transmetalation of gallium (III) halides with n-butyllithium. GaAs nanocrystals might then be produced by the reaction of Mg3As2 with the elemental gallium species [243].

InP QDs are also a strong contender for replacing or outperforming CdSe nanocrystals in specific applications, like photodynamic treatment and bio-imaging [253]. Regarding emission color, InP nanocrystals can match or even surpass CdSe QDs. Interestingly, it has been shown that InP has little intrinsic toxicity, in contrast to cadmium, selenium, or arsenic. Additionally, structurally stable, InP nanocrystals improve optical stability in biological systems [254]. More research has been done on synthesizing InP QDs about other III–V QDs [255]. According to recent studies, InP QDs were created by thermalizing the InP precursor in a heated mixture containing TOPO and TOP [256]. Due to the high reactivity of phosphorous precursors like [P(TMS)3], it has been demonstrated that a dehalosilylation reaction can be used for InP QD synthesis. However, this reaction is challenging to maintain because the ultrarapid nucleation consumes almost all phosphorous precursors [257,258]. Moreover, poor size distributions are formed due to the unregulated nucleation mechanism [259].

Various indium halides were traditionally employed as precursors, and TOP functioned as a solvent, stabilizing agent, and supplier of phosphorus [243]. Carboxylate ligands, like phosphine, have been used instead of phosphorous precursors to slow down the nucleation process and keep the reaction under control. Due to this strategy's reduced nucleation, narrowly sized InP QDs were produced, which allowed for the post-nucleation development of some unreacted precursors [260].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization methods for quantum dot nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: Several variables can be altered with QD synthesis, including the molar proportion of reactants, the reaction temperature, the resulting medium's pH, precursor concentration, and reaction time. The structure and size-dependent capabilities of the nanostructures can be influenced by each of these factors [261]. To identify the exact dimension distribution of QDs, a variety of approaches can be used, including UV-vis absorption spectroscopy, small-angle X-ray scattering [262], transmission electron microscopy with high resolution (TEM), and Raman scattering [263]. Sadly, there are still problems with these techniques, including poor resolution [264], laborious data processing [265], lengthy sample setup [266], and scarce instrument accessibility. The nanocrystal size affects the color of the QD emission, but the breadth of the PL peak depends on the nanocrystal size distribution. Based on prior investigations, correlation fluorescence spectra might thus provide the maximum diameter of QDs [267,268].

Electrons are transported by the tiny, artificially produced semiconductor particles known as quantum dots. These particles emit different light colors when they come into contact with ultraviolet (UV) radiation. Due to their unique features, quantum dots are employed in many other applications, such as biological labeling, solar cells, composites, drug creation, and imaging. An overview of the techniques used to characterize quantum dot nanomaterials is presented in Fig. 6 .

Quantum dots are tiny regions of a suitable substance implanted in semiconductors or other matrices. The capacity to arrange these QDs into complex arrays implies many technological exploration and application opportunities. High-density arrays of QDs were made possible by the significant feat of producing lattice-mismatched heterostructures (also called the "Stranski-Krastanow" growth mode) epitaxially, such as vertically stacked InAs islands on a GaAs substrate [269–272]. QD structures have much to offer regarding technology, especially for active and passive optoelectronic devices (VCSELs or vertical-cavity surface-emitting lasers). However, depending on the growth techniques employed, namely molecular beam epitaxy (MBE) and metal-organic chemical vapor phase deposition (MOCVD), the islands differ in size, morphology, chemical composition, and lattice strain [273].

Within quantum wells and quantum dot structures, the nanoscale confinement of electrons determines and shapes many of the characteristics of optoelectronic devices. Essential features of QDs that control optical properties, light emission and absorption, lasing efficiency, and other optoelectronic device properties are the shape, size, and strain field of individual QDs, as well as the quality, density, and homogeneity of equitised and equipped dot arrangements [274,275].

Their structural features significantly influence the future possible applications of QDs, such as controlled-NOT for Qbits in quantum computing [276]. Since the first photo pumping [277] and injection lasing [278] demonstrations, a plethora of QD studies have been carried out, offering some insight into the structural properties required to obtain strong wave function confinement and a sufficiently sharp distribution of the electron density (cf. and the contributions in this volume) [270]. A critical minimum QD size is required for the dot to hold at least one electron or exciton. A critical maximum QD size is linked to the essential separation of the energy levels for thermally induced decoupling. To ensure the coupling of states between QDs, the QD size has to be uniform. The composition and strain of the QDs also influence the stability and localization of states. This also affects the pre-ordering of QDs by surface structuring due to steps or monolayer growth along neighboring surfaces, vertical stacking on top of or between buried dots, and self-organized formation and lateral arrangement (cf., e.g.) [279–281]. In the end, expanding the emission range toward longer wavelengths will require a better understanding and control of controlled growth through lattice mismatched hetero-structures or self-assembling phenomena (see, e.g.) [281].

The growth, self-assembly, and physical characteristics of quantum dots are studied using a broad range of imaging techniques, including energy-filtered transmission electron microscopy (EFTEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM), scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM), and others [282–285]. Among these are the plan view and the cross-section HREM. TEM imaging techniques can directly assess nanoscale structures' strain field, size, form, and related defects. This is especially true when using electron diffraction contrast imaging (DC) with bright field (BF) and dark field (DF) modes [286].

Electron microscope imaging is the only direct method of structure examination that can sufficiently discern capped QDs without destroying the hidden dots. (See, for example, applications in the systems CdZnSe [287], InGaN [288], GalnP [289], Si-Si02 [287], GeSi [290], GeSiC [291], AIGaAs [292], InGaAs [293], InAsP [289], and InGaAs [289]). By using a phenomenological TEM analysis, it is possible to verify whether the dots have formed in the strain field of buried dots, whether the dot height is proportional to the material deposited, whether the QD composition is smooth or inhomogeneous, whether the strains vary with the dot layer spacing, and whether the QD dispersion is bimodal or varies with stacking number. Due to the practical difficulties with in-situ examinations and the problems in producing suitable clear HREM and TEM samples, the development process can only be directly examined if particular ultrahigh vacuum (UHV) growth conditions are established [288]. Not only is XSTM, the cross-section variant of STM, unable to reach buried dots, but TEM needs to have the sensitivity to do a composition analysis by itself. Therefore, several attempts have been made to determine variables like composition and strain using more accurate quantitative HREM techniques, primarily utilizing image Fourier filtering or lattice fringe distortion analysis (see Sect. 3.2.4). However, only when all of the previously mentioned methods are coupled with electron diffraction techniques (like the convergent beam electron diffraction, CBED) and X-ray, electron, and optical spectroscopy methods (like photoluminescence, cathodoluminescence, Raman spectroscopy, etc.) will a comprehensive structural investigation be feasible [294]. The HREM and TEM processes need help understanding the picture elements concerning the structures they are studying, both phenomenologically and statistically. Distinguishing strain from shape-related contrast features is especially difficult. Applying theoretical contrast modeling and image matching is necessary to determine the experimentally non-recoverable data and produce a unique structural analysis [295].

However, the simulation of HREM and TEM images of objects scaled down to nanometers requires atomic-scale structure modeling appropriate for microscopic relevant dimensions. The following modeling of HREM images based on such models reveals the strain field surrounding the QDs. This led to a notable modification of the contrast of the electron microscope image. The primary distinctions in the image contrast between relaxed and unrelaxed QDs are a virtual truncation of the QD shapes and strain-induced black-white lobes in cross-sectional HREM images (cf. one of the first papers using molecular static energy minimization with many-body cluster potentials for the modeling and image simulation of pyramidal-shaped QDs in the InGaAs-system [296]. This could be the cause of the rounded spots that are commonly seen in cross-sectional imaging. It was also unable to calculate the plan-view photos in [297] for technical reasons. However, since the diffraction contrast of the cross-sectional orientation was simulated, the promise and limitations of size and shape analysis could be discussed along with the depth dependence of comparison in the pyramids. This highlights the importance of proceeding with the utmost caution when analyzing the photos. In contrast to these results, several papers [298–300] emphasize the same caution and the necessity of adequate models and picture simulations.

A very flexible and helpful method for identifying and characterizing basic processes in various biological, inorganic, and inorganic systems. In most of these applications, it is beneficial to use spectrally and temporally well-defined sources, even though the exact quantum statistics of the stimulating light are often immaterial. Nonetheless, nonclassical characteristics like antibunching, entanglement, squeezing, etc., may emerge if spontaneous emission from excited systems is adequately described [301]. The research indicates that these kinds of effects occur when the qualities of matter and light cannot be factorized. Put another way, correlations between the quantum mechanical characteristics of the matter excitations and the quantum statistics of the generated light influence the latter.

Time-resolved and static UV-vis spectral measurements were carried out to understand the solution structure and cluster formation dynamics [302]. An Olis rapid scan monochromator (RSM) equipped with a Hitachi 3501 spectrophotometer. The Olis RSM spectrophotometer collected 300–550 nm spectral area spectra at 1–10 ms intervals. As a function of time, we saw the frequencies and spectral strength. The static electronic absorption and extinction spectra were obtained using a Hitachi 3501 spectrophotometer, both with and without an integrating sphere attachment. All the spectra were measured in the 1000-185 nm region with a resolution of 1 nm [303].

Since UV-VIS and photoluminescence spectroscopy are fast, non-destructive, and contactless techniques, they are frequently employed to analyze QDs optically. Hazdra et al. confirmed deposited QD structures using photo-modulated reflectance spectroscopy, an experimental method that is rarely used. This method probes a broader range of critical points and offers comparable energy resolution to photoluminescence at low temperatures. It highlights the ground state and numerous high-order interband optical transitions that can be used to infer band structure, particularly of the wetting layer [304].

As was previously said, QD size plays a significant role in establishing the photoluminescence's spectrum location and purity. It may also fine-tune QDs' optical characteristics, such as their fluorescence [305]. Conventional methods such as dynamic light scattering (DLS) research, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) are frequently used to estimate the size of QDs. Using photoluminescence, photoluminescence excitation, and Raman scattering spectroscopies in conjunction with a model of photoluminescence and LO phonon energies, Gu and colleagues computed the size and composition of optically active CdZnSe/ZnBeSe QDs [306]. Apart from these techniques, field flow fractionation has also been applied successfully as an excellent adjunct method to the standard equipment used for water-soluble QD characterization [306].

The size of epitaxially produced QDs is routinely tracked using various characterization techniques, including TEM, atomic force microscopy (AFM), and, preferably, scanning tunneling microscopy (STM). Additionally, researchers used magneto-tunneling tests to measure the size of InAs QDs and found that the results closely matched the sizes found by AFM and TEM [307]. Yamauchi's work examined quantum size effects on the electrical structure of InAs and InGaAs QDs and their morphologies using scanning tunneling spectroscopy. According to Lees et al., it is also essential since surface chemistry influences the final hydrodynamic diameter of QDs. Therefore, a technique for gathering qualitative data regarding this metric must be created [308]. The surface chemistry of QDs has been described using various methods, such as X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy, and Rutherford backscattering. Lees et al. employed analytical ultracentrifugation to assess organic dyes' size distribution and surface chemistry (ODs). This technique was susceptible to nanocrystal size, detecting variations in nanocrystal sizes down to one lattice plane [309].

Although discretized electronic levels are possible within a quantum dot, the surrounding semiconductor matrix introduces a continuum of accessible conduction band states and filled valence states. As a result, the environment around a quantum dot can generate an ensemble of electron-hole pairs in the presence of an optical field [310]. Most of these pairs recombine quickly to produce photons at the bandgap energy of the semiconductor. Still, occasionally, an electron-hole combination may get trapped inside the distinct exciton levels of the quantum dot. For this to happen, the excess energy of the excitons must be eliminated via carrier scattering or phonons [311].

Determining the exact mechanism of this relaxation and its dependence on external parameters like temperature and magnetic field has been a significant area of research on quantum dots, especially in the 1990s. Tens of picoseconds is a relatively short timescale for this relaxing process, which removes any coherence with the excitation laser [312]. Therefore, exciton production in a quantum dot via bandgap excitation can typically be addressed using an incoherent driving field between quantum dot states. Moreover, spin orientations of the optically produced electrons and holes are affected during the relaxation processes, resulting in only a residual association with the polarisation of the original excitation laser. Consequently, the radiative recombination of an electron-hole pair in the quantum dot provides information on the quantized energy levels and the optical selection criteria. As such, one of the essential tools for quantum dot research throughout the years has been measuring the spectrum of quantum dot light emission under continuous stimulation, or micro photoluminescence (NPL) [313–316].

When examining nanoscale features in solids, electron microscope techniques offer handy tools, particularly when combined with traditional transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution electron microscopy (HREM) [317]. The fluorescence of quantum dots is quite persistent. It is impervious to a variety of chemical processes, such as solvent dehydration, epoxy resin embedding, and heat polymerization (between 60 and 70 degrees Celsius) (Giepmens et al., 2005) [318]. Suppose post-fixation with osmium tetroxide is avoided (which instantly and permanently kills their fluorescence). In that case, this characteristic allows specimens to be prepared in which the fluorescence can be maintained during processing for electron microscopy [319]. These specimens can be set in epoxy resin, but LR (London Resin) White acrylic resin works better because it has less autofluorescence than the former. Using optical sectioning, specimens in formed resin can be examined using confocal or multiphoton microscopy (for cells and tissue slices) or conventional epifluorescence (for cells) [320]. Ultramicrotomy is a way to cut conventionally ultrathin sections, which can then be put on copper electron microscope (EM) grids and examined again using fluorescence microscopy. The same object can be studied at a higher resolution using electron microscopy [321]. With this method, specimens can be analyzed in a somewhat large field using light microscopy, and then a smaller region of focus can be directly correlatedly imaged using electron microscopy. Currently being investigated are protocols and reagents that might be appropriate substitutes for osmium tetroxide post-fixation to preserve better and contrast cell membranes while maintaining quantum-dot fluorescence [322,323]. This method could collect and store specimens with stable fluorescence at room temperature for future use in light and electron microscopy analyses. Creating patient tissue libraries from these specimens would be valuable in comparative toxicological pathological studies [324]. These specimens will be significant in protein expression and localization investigations in healthy versus pathological tissue. According to, quantum dots are already starting to contribute to molecular pathology by helping to evaluate several malignant tumor indicators [325]. They can further apply this to experimental pathology and toxicology by automating picture acquisition and increasing their multiplexing capacity [326].

Surface characterization techniques are crucial for comprehending quantum dot nanomaterials' chemical and physical attributes [327]. Due to their diminutive size, quantum dots (QDs) include many surface atoms concerned with core atoms. This results in the surface atoms exerting a significant influence not only on the characteristics of QDs but also on the reactivity of the entire particle. According to Table 2 , the proportion of surface atoms significantly rises when the particle size is below 10 nm. When the particle size decreases to 1 nm, the surface atomic percentage will be about 90 %, with most atoms localized on the surface of quantum dots (QDs) [328].

Hydrophobic interactions mainly occur between the nonpolar sections of organic adsorbates and NMs. Carbon QDs, which also possess nonpolar surfaces, exhibit comparable behavior [330]. Therefore, the aromatic parts of organic ligands can be loosely attached to the surfaces of carbon nanomaterials due to hydrophobic effects [331]. The reduced adsorption of organic ligands on the carbon NM surface at higher pH levels indicates that the ionization of functional groups diminishes hydrophobic effects, demonstrating that this interaction is influenced by pH. π–π interactions can occur between organic ligands with aromatic moiety (or C=C double bonds) and the π electrons of benzene rings on the surface of NMs, such as carbon QDs [332]. The interaction between π and π electrons can be affected by the number of conjugated groups and the relative arrangement of aromatic rings of adsorption products to benzene rings on carbon NM substrates [333]. Surface anion chelation refers to the direct bidentate contact between the quantum dots (QDs) surface and the organic adsorbate ligand [334]. Surface metal ions through the two oxygen atoms of surrounding phenol groups [335].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optoelectronic device applications of quantum dot nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QDs) are minuscule semiconductor particles, often measuring only a few nanometers in size. In contrast to bulk semiconductor materials, they display distinct electrical and optical properties. Like genuine atoms, quantum dots (QDs) have discrete electronic states, and their electronic wave function is mainly similar to that of an actual atom. Because of this, they are commonly referred to as artificial atoms. QDs' unique characteristics have made them useful in many contemporary technologies, such as solar cells, light-emitting diodes, field-effect transistors, photodetectors, and biological systems [336–345]. Over the past few decades, QD-based optoelectronics has developed incredibly fast [346].

Many years have passed since the initial research on semiconductors' reduced dimensionality gave rise to "artificial atoms," or quantum dots (QDs). With diameters measured in nanometers, these semiconductor nanocrystals display quantum scale effects in their optical and electrical characteristics. In particular, several QD material systems are widely realized today concerning core-shell architectures and tunable and efficient photoluminescence (PL) with narrow emission and photochemical stability. Because of this, QDs are being used as active components in a broad range of gadgets and uses. Many of these uses, like QD-based displays, are commercially accessible and part of our everyday lives [347–350]. Fig. 7 demonstrates the types of Optoelectrical device applications.

QD displays, which have the potential to surpass current display technologies, will be the next big thing in the display business. Numerous massive television manufacturing companies have already released various QD-embedded display brands.

All items that claim to have QD displays are used for backlighting instead of being integrated into the display. With a yellow phosphor coating, white LEDs, which backlight the display screen in conventional LCD TVs, create a wide range of colors. As a result, displays are less effective at producing vivid colors. When placed between the rear and the LCD panel, the QDs can directly transform blue LED light into extremely pure narrow-band red and green hues.

Some display technologies were developed in the latter part of the 20th century, as seen in Fig. 8 . The light source for each pixel (sub-pixel) in an emissive display is adjustable. The content of the image being shown determines the pixel transmission status in non-emissive displays, which use an external light source.

This makes a big difference for the observer because the color palette has more vitality, with brighter greens and reds appearing deeper. Furthermore, it's estimated that compared to earlier models, these displays' power usage was cut by up to 20 % [351–353].

The next big step would be fabricating QD-enabled or QLEDs to replace conventional LEDs in the backlight. The primary distinction is that these QLEDs' light-emitting components are QDs; their technology is comparable to that of OLEDs. A layer of QDs separates the layers that transport holes and electrons. When an electric field is provided, electrons and holes go toward the QD layer, recombining to release photons. It is possible to adjust the emission light to a particular wavelength when needed [354–356].

Colvin and colleagues created a new class of photonic devices from the 1994 design of the first QD-based light-emitting diode (QD-LED). These early CdSe QD-based L EDs demonstrated less external quantum efficiency around three orders of magnitude than more recent QD-based LED examples. The discovery led to a substantial rise in the QD-LED efficiency attribute [357–359].

LEDs primarily inject electrons and holes into the device's cathode and anode using a direct-current voltage. When the carriers are recombined in the active layer, the excess energy is released as light, a process known as electroluminescence [360,361].

QD LEDs fall into two primary categories: electroluminescent LEDs, in which the QDs are directly excited by current flowing through a film containing QDs, and optically excited emitters, in which a conventional epitaxial blue or UV LED drives a layer of QD-doped material applied over the emitter chip or near the device's output. To achieve a balanced emission color by lighting or display requirements, QDs may be combined with other fluorescent materials, such as rare earth ions, for white light emission. These devices are intended for use in areas like outdoor or room illumination, computer display backlighting, and other applications with slightly differing spectrum needs [362–373]. Fig. 9 illustrates the types of LEDs.

The initial research on QD phosphors that emit white light for display applications focused on developing organic solvent-compatible QDs. The idea was to add these QDs to an organic solvent-soluble polymer binder for film formation or casting as monoliths. These could then be packaged with a conventional epitaxial LED chip that emits blue or UV light. There were two basic ways to get white light: either using dual color emission from multi-shell heterostructures like CdSe/ZnS/CdSe/ZnS as reported by Sapra et al., or using surface-related emission from "trap-rich" QDs like Cds. Broad emission spectrum trap-rich QDs can also form in water, even though both instances discussed were created in organic solvents.

Despite the trap-rich nature of the CdS, the PL QY was only 17 %, whereas the dual color emitter's total integrated PL QY was more significant at 30 %. Since the inner ZnS shell was thick enough to separate the core CdSe from the outside CdSe well, the latter released light at approximately 500 and 612 nm. The respective strengths of the two components could be adjusted by heat treatment post-synthesis, allowing the warmth of the resulting emission to be precisely calibrated around the white area of the CIE color diagram. To create green/yellow phosphors, Jang et al. combined red-emitting CdSe QDs with the Ce phosphor, Sr3SiO5:Ce3+, and Li+ doping [374,375].

The resulting combination QD/Ce3+ emitter covered the output face of a blue-emitting InGaN chip, producing white light with a color rendering index (CRI) of 90.1 and a luminous efficiency of 14 lm W1 [376].

The majority of the development of white light phosphor utilizing water-based materials has focused on CdTe QDs, which can be synthesized with high PL QY and in huge reaction volumes that are easily scalable to production levels, in contrast to the previous research that used QDs synthesized in organic solvents. Finding a water-compatible host into which to incorporate aqueously grown QD materials is difficult because doing so would prevent degradation from surface trap formation or aggregate tendency, which would make it difficult for FRET to transfer excitons to nearby QDs in the ensemble with low PL QY [377].

Electroluminescent QD LEDs have been developed using both aqueous and organic solvent-grown QDs. Early devices, including those described by Colvin et al., used CdSe QDs in combination with PPV films, which served as a hole transport layer. This was a significant borrowing from the field of light-emitting polymers. Depending on the drive voltage, emission from both layers—green from the PPV and yellow from the QDs—with charge accumulation of each kind on opposite sides of the PPV/CdSe heterojunction could be achieved. The PPV is closest to the ITO anode, while the QDs are closest to the Mg cathode. These early devices had little air stability and low external quantum efficiency (EQE, 0.001–0.01 %). Gao et al. created EL QD LEDs on ITO substrates with an Al top electrode by using water-soluble CdSe QDs grown with the lactic acid ligands and a water-soluble precursor to PPV acting as a polyelectrolyte [378,379].

A particular light frequency irradiating the p-n junction in solar cells produces electron-hole pairs. Focused on the p-type semiconductor are the holes that are created. In the meantime, a photo-generated voltage is produced by the electrons moving to the n-type semiconductor. A current is made when there is an external circuit—alternatively known as the photovoltaic effect, this conversion. Power conversion efficiency (PCE) of solar cells based on inorganic and organometallic perovskites was compared by Hodes and colleagues in 2015 [380–382].

Quantum dots (QDs) of colloidal semiconductors are nanoscale crystals floating in a solution. Optoelectronic devices, imaging, and fundamental science find them attractive due to their easily tunable qualities, ability to be processed in solution, and distinctive photophysics. Because they directly regulate the material's energy levels (i.e., the gap or band gap between the highest occupied molecular orbital to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital, or HOMO-LUMO), the size and size distribution of the QD ensemble is essential [383]. Quantum dots have also been utilized in energy harvesting, employing novel device structures that exploit their distinct characteristics. Quantum dot solar cells include nanocrystals in their structure to improve light absorption and separate photogenerated charges [384,385]. Quantum dots possess absorption characteristics that vary with their size, enabling the development of multi-junction solar cells. By arranging many layers of quantum dots with varying bandgap energies, a wider range of sunlight may be absorbed, leading to a substantial improvement in the efficiency of converting solar energy. These cutting-edge solar cells can enhance the efficiency and cost-efficiency of renewable energy systems [386]. Table 3 displays statistics on the performance of quantum dot solar cells with different core materials. It highlights important measurements such as efficiency and the range of wavelengths.

The fantastic ability of quantum dot-sensitized solar cells (QDSSCs) to grow sunshine and produce many electron/hole pairs, along with their ease of manufacture and low cost, make them an essential energy source. Unfortunately, their power conversion efficiency (4 %) is often lower than dye-sensitized solar cells' (≤12 %); this is primarily because of their small absorption regions and the charge recombination that occurs at the interfaces between the TiO2 electrolyte and quantum dot/electrolyte [388]. Fig. 10 shows us the generation of Solar cells.

The photodetector's operating mechanism is comparable to solar cells [389]. The lack of widely utilized detectors for the near-infrared spectral area (NIR), such as Si-based avalanche photodiodes (APD) or CCD cameras used in the visible range, is the primary driving force behind the substantial development of QD-based photodetectors. High-sensitivity NIR photodetectors, like InGaAs-based arrays, Ge or InGaAs APDs, and photomultiplier tubes (PMTs), are prohibitively expensive, which prevents their widespread use. According to this theory, soluble-processed QDs could take the place of inorganic semiconductors that are already in use. There are numerous opportunities for developing these QD-based photodetectors thanks to surface chemistry and bandgap adjustment.

Most PbS and PbSe, narrow-band gap semiconductors, are the foundation for NIR photodetectors. Lead chalcogenide QD-based photodetectors with exceptionally high operating characteristics may now be fabricated because of recent developments in QD synthesis, air stability enhancements, and novel surface treatment ideas. They work well in all kinds of photodetector configurations, including phototransistors, photodiodes, and photoconductors, and they typically have very high gain and sensitivity or quick photo response times. PbS QD-based photodetectors, for example, can have a detective of up to 1014 Jones, bandwidths of up to 3 MHz, and sensitivity in an extensive spectral range of 350 to 2400 nm [390–397]. Fig. 11 shows the types of Photodetectors.

These days, mid-infrared (MIR) photodetectors are often built using narrow-bandgap semiconductors produced on a suitable substrate by molecular beam epitaxy or chemical vapor deposition. These expensive and intricate technological processes limit MIR photodetectors' broad range of applications. Numerous substances based on mercury have been suggested as superior PbS replacements in MIR detection. The HgTe colloidal QD-based photoconductors that Guyot-Sionnest's group has described have photocurrent spectra that fall between 1 and 5 mm.

HgTe QD-based photoconductor devices have been shown by Chen et al. to have both excellent responsiveness and a rapid temporal response. The spray-coated device, made of a HgTe QD monolayer, demonstrated a 3 dB bandwidth of up to 1 MHz. According to the scientists, there are more opportunities to enhance photodetection in the MIR by adjusting the surface chemistry and QD size. This allows for control over the photodetector's time response and gain. Using intraband transitions presents another possibility for using quantum dots to detect MIR radiation. This idea was first developed for 2D nanostructures, and many MIR photodetectors were created.

Lhuillier et al. have also reported on an n-doped HgSe QD-based photodetector for the 3–20 mm range. The solids formed of 5–40 nm QDs were demonstrated to have extremely high charge mobility. This apparatus showed excellent detectivity and performed well at ambient temperature. MIR detectors based on intraband transitions in colloidal QDs may benefit from additional advancements through precise control of QD doping and surface chemistry [398–406].

UV visible-blind photodetectors are crucial for solar astronomy, skin cancer prevention, combustion monitoring, and other applications. Wide-band inorganic semiconductors are the best candidate material for UV photodetector production because of their great sensitivity and lack of need for additional expensive optical filters.

Nevertheless, it is expensive to fabricate UV photodetectors with conventional semiconductor technology. Wide-bandgap semiconductors are being replaced by solution-processed QDs, which have more excellent selectivity and gain than bulk semiconductors. ZnO, which is ecologically safe, is still the most often used semiconductor compound for UV light detection. Other compounds that can be employed include SnO2, NbO5, MgO, ZnS, and MgO. ZnO QDs have been tried using various materials to produce the best-performing UV photodetectors. Kim et al. used a p-type NiO layer and an n-type ZnO QD layer to create an all-inorganic p–n heterojunction UV detector.
