ZnO QD-based photodetectors have lately shown extraordinarily high performance, as shown by Guo et al. With a hybrid active layer made of ZnO QDs and hole-conducting semi-conducting polymers (P3HT and PVK), they could detect at room temperature at 360 nm 3.4 *10^15 Jones. It is important to note that flexible photodetectors are essential for creating portable optoelectronic devices and are significant for any spectral region. Currently, several fabrication techniques are being explored. In addition, pure graphene quantum dots (GQDs) offer low-cost solution processability and could be a high-performing substitute for bulk semiconductors. Additionally, Kim et al. reported obtaining a photo response in a broad spectral range, spanning from UV to NIR, by sandwiching GQDs between two graphene sheets [407–419]. There is a considerable morphological reliance of the Al NSs on the optical characteristics of the heterostructures, and the Al/ZnO heterostructures show better light usage than the ZnO/Al heterostructures. Carrier movement greatly benefits from the inter-diffusion of Al atoms into ZnO matrixes. An exceptional photoresponsivity of 11.98 A W−1 is thus attained under 6.9 mW cm−2 UV light illumination at 10 V bias, and the optimal photocurrent of the Al/ZnO heterostructure photodetectors is thus greatly enhanced by 275 times to ∼1.065 mA in comparison to that of the pristine ZnO device. Moreover, a comparatively quick reaction is also seen with the Al/ZnO devices, opening the way for the creation of high-performing UV photodetectors for use (Fig. 12 ).

The ability of QDs to provide narrow fluorescence and specific hues due to their configurable bandgap opens up possibilities for biological applications. Labeled fluorescent dyes for identifying particular proteins, antibodies, or cells are necessary for many molecular biology and biotechnology biological research. These dyes help in the detection process by chemically attaching to the targeted molecule. In this sense, QDs are becoming increasingly well-liked since they outperform conventional dyes regarding color contrast, brightness, and in vivo stability [420].

By carefully adjusting the synthesis conditions and material combinations, QDs in the desired size distribution can now be produced thanks to substantial research on colloidal synthesis techniques [421–423]. Consequently, it is possible to create QDs with high quantum yield and fluorescence in any range from the near UV to the near-infrared. The ability of QD labels to absorb photons from a broad range of wavelengths, from ultraviolet to long visible wavelengths, is one of their competitive advantages. This enables the simultaneous, signal-overlapping stimulation of many QD sizes by a single light source. Conversely, each organic dye has a unique excitation wavelength due to its narrow absorption band. While the emission peaks are broader and nonsymmetrical, QDs exhibit a narrow fluorescence emission with a symmetric peak. Compared to organic dyes, QDs are considerably brighter regarding emission, more photostable, and resistant to photobleaching.

This makes photographing thick specimens in vivo simple. Despite these benefits, studies are being conducted to assess the potential toxicity of heavy metals and the in vivo UV excitation of QDs [424,425].

Carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen make up the majority of carbon quantum dots (C-dots), which demonstrated outstanding structure-tunable optical characteristics. Numerous kinds of solid-state optical devices have made use of them. It is difficult to generate high quantum yield and large Stokes shift C-dots using straightforward and controlled methods because of the photoluminescence quenching brought on by aggregation [426]. Due to quantum confinement, quantum dots, which are semiconductor nanocrystals, exhibit size-tunable optical characteristics [427]. Carbon quantum dots (C-dots) are not made of heavy metals like quantum dots are; instead, they are made of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen [428,429]. Both the core structure and the functional surface groups of C-dots influence their optical characteristics, providing a simple way to adjust their functional characteristics [430]. Because of their low cost, ease of synthesis, and adjustable optical and electrical properties, C-dots have found widespread use in a variety of optoelectronic devices, such as luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs), fluorescent anticounterfeiting codes, light-emitting diodes (LEDs), catalysis, biomedical sensors, and more [431–433]. Vacuum heating was used to create the blue and green C-dots, whereas solvothermal synthesis was used to create the orange C-dots. With a significant Stokes shift (0.37–0.73 eV), the as-prepared B-, G-, and O-C-dots had QYs of 90 %, 73 %, and 72 %, respectively. The best LSC (100 cm2) with a multilayer structure demonstrated an exterior optical efficiency of 9.1 % and a power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 6.0 % by optimizing the concentration of the C-dots in each layer. These results are greater than those of the majority of the reported LSCs. According to the models, optimal multilayer LSCs may theoretically have an exterior optical efficiency of more than 12 % for devices that are 1 m2 in size. The as-prepared multilayer LSCs are appropriate for possible commercial BIPVs due to their excellent optical efficiency, high Color Rendering Index (above 80), and high average visible transmission (above 70 %) [434]. Using polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP) polymer as a matrix, the large-area LSC (15 × 15 × 0.5 cm3) demonstrated an external optical efficiency of 2.2 % (uncharacterized spectrum, 60 mW cm−2, under natural solar irradiation). The LSC demonstrated a power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 1.13 % when exposed to natural sunlight (20 mW cm−2, uncharacterized spectrum) upon connection to silicon solar cells. Optical spectroscopy demonstrated that these remarkable outcomes were achieved by totally reducing the reabsorption losses during light collecting. These results open the door for the lab-to-fab transfer of these devices by showing that scalable manufacturing processes may produce large-area, eco-friendly, high-efficiency LSCs [435]. The interaction between citric acid and urea is made more selective by the addition of boric acid, which has a strong bond with urea. Biuret, cyanuric acid (triuret), and a crosslinked octa-cyclic molecule can be used to dominate the synthesis of in situ manufactured C-dots, which benefit from the high reaction selectivity and spatial confinement growth of C-dots in porous matrices. The resulting C-dots/matrix combination showed outstanding thermal and photostability, as well as vivid green emission with a quantum yield of up to 90 %. White light-emitting diodes (LEDs) with a color rendering index of 84 and a luminous efficiency of 88.14 lm W−1 are fabricated using the as-prepared C-dots as a proof-of-concept, demonstrating significant promise for LED applications [436]. Different methods have been used to create CQDs. The majority of research focuses on each synthesis scheme to determine the most effective, eco-friendly, and high-yielding technique. The two main techniques used to create CQDs are the top-down and bottom-up approaches. The process of dissolving larger carbonaceous materials or structures using techniques including chemical oxidation, discharge, electrochemical oxidation, and ultrasonic treatments is known as the top-down methodology (Fig. 2) [437]. However, this approach has several drawbacks, including the need for expensive components, strict reaction conditions, and a lengthy reaction time [438]. The bottom-up approach, on the other hand, entails converting small carbon structures into the right size CQDs. Hydrothermal treatment, ultrasonic treatment, thermal breakdown, pyrolysis, carbonization, microwave synthesis, and solvothermal methods are examples of common bottom-up methods for creating CQDs (Fig. 13 ) [439].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Advantages and challenges in quantum dot-based optoelectronics

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QDs) have generated substantial interest due to their unique properties, including high quantum efficiency, high color purity, low-cost solution processability, and an easily tunable emission wavelength. They are considered alternative emissive materials for next-generation light-emitting diodes (LEDs) in lighting and display applications [441]. Quantum dots (QDs) have many benefits in their display application, such as excellent illumination efficiency and color rendering, low cost, and mass production capability. They are also friendly to the environment [442].

QD-based light-emitting devices have shown great promise for future commercialization. Their performance has been significantly improved through the optimization of QD engineering and device architecture to address the issues of nonradiative recombination, energy transfer, and field-induced quenching in the devices [443]. QD-LED-based technology is the best option for next-generation displays because of its potential benefits in response time, efficiency, power consumption, contrast ratio, and longevity.

Micro-LED (μLED) display technology has recently surfaced as a potential display technology. Many scientists worldwide believe this technology is the best option for displays in the future. μLEDs' self-emitting mechanism and inorganic material properties combine to make them a viable alternative to traditional display technologies. μLED displays are a widespread research issue because of their superior performance, which has been predicted and even shown at the research level regarding luminance, efficiency, power consumption, contrast ratio, lifetime, and response time. Wearable watches, smartphones, head-up displays in cars, AR/VR, mini projectors, and high-end televisions are just a few of the uses for μLED displays [444–446].

To use μLEDs as a display, we need to have an array that emits the primary colors of the RGB (red, green, and blue). Using mass-transfer methods, RGB LED matrices can be constructed from full-color μLED displays. There are several significant disadvantages to producing colored μLEDs simultaneously employing primary color μLED chips. First, because the low-efficiency emission creates a green gap between other wavelengths, no material is suitable for growing effective green-emitting LEDs. Second, different substrates and operating conditions are typically used to produce LEDs with varied colors. As a result, their integration requires expensive, intricate circuitry. The issues above and numerous other noteworthy obstacles, including low transfer yield, sluggish transfer time, expensive fabrication, and challenges with inspection and repair, restrict the use of the method above to realize full-color LEDs.

A method to achieve full-color LEDs is to mix single-color LEDs with a specific color-converting substance, commonly a phosphor. Phosphorus-assisted color down-conversion is when a phosphor substance absorbs short wavelengths and, following energy down-conversion, re-emits wavelengths that are somewhat longer for LEDs. Typically, a blue or violet LED uses a broad, yellow-emitting phosphor.

The ratios of direct emissions from the LED and phosphor material and the phosphor composition can be adjusted to control the colors emitted. Y3Al5O12: Ce3+ (YAG: Ce) yellow emitting phosphor combined with nitride-based single-color LEDs is a standard method for creating industrial white LED sources. Because of their low energy consumption, this white LED (WLED) type is the most popular type for lighting applications. YAG: The range of applications for Ce yellow phosphor-based white LEDs is still limited by severe issues like poor color quality and low spectral efficiency. In recent years, optically pumped QDs have emerged as a novel and exciting class of phosphors. QD-incorporated LEDs, also known as QD-LEDs, are used in several technologies, including self-emissive displays, display backlights, and general lighting. Regarding color quality, efficiency, and adaptability in optoelectronic systems, QDs have much to offer [447–449].

Displays made with QDs and μLEDs are sometimes referred to as QD‐μLED displays since a better resolution requires the use of LEDs, or μLEDs, with a size smaller than 100 μm. A full-color display made possible by an ultra-violet (UV) LED and colloidal QDs (CQDs) was reported by Han et al. in 2015. Three primary hues, red, green, and blue, were mixed with CQDs to create GaN-based UV LEDs with a 40 μm pitch. Using an aerosol jet (AJ) printer, RGB QDs were deposited on the UV LED array to enable non-contact liquid deposition, including functional ingredients and more accurate, mask-less fine printing. The spraying chamber and the ultrasonic atomizer are the two primary components of the AJ system. Ultrasonic oscillations are used inside the atomizer to atomize the QD suspension. The generated aerosol is then moved via the nitrogen gas flow to the spray chamber's nozzle. Next, the AJ printer's nozzle produces high-resolution, narrow-linewidth QDs on the substrate. A DBR coating was applied to the device's top to improve UV light use, reflecting UV light leaks to the QD layers. Compared to the samples without DBR, the luminous flux of blue, green, and red was increased by 194 %, 173 %, and 183 %, respectively. Under varied current conditions, the radiation's luminous efficacy was reported to be 165 lm/Watt. Every emissive part functioned as a pixel, emitting three sub-pixels, each of the colors red, green, and blue. Lin et al. applied a photoresist (PR) mold between them to minimize optical cross-talk between layers of each distinct color 2017. This further enhanced the device. The emission efficiencies of blue, red, and green QDs were increased by 5 %, 23 %, and 32 %, respectively, indicating the utility of this technology. Additionally, it was discovered that PR mold was beneficial in lessening the coffee-ring effect, which causes the uneven distribution of QDs on the LED surface. This approach will probably help the next generation of light-emitting gadgets [450–452]. The best option for next-generation displays is QD-LED-based technology due to its substantial potential benefits in efficiency, power consumption, contrast ratio, lifetime, and response time.

The potential for nanotoxicity in different QD-based materials still severely restricts their range of applications, especially those related to daily life. Despite recent improvements in their production, the toxicity and biological behavior of new Cd-free QDs have yet to be thoroughly explored. As a result, a significant portion of any future effort on QD development must be devoted to examining QD-induced toxicity and biocompatibility.

The long-term environmental stability of numerous QD-based systems in ambient circumstances still needs to be better known and studied. New approaches to improve the long-term stability and longevity of QDs must consequently be developed. In light of their use in electronic devices, their strength, safety, and the decommissioning and disposal of equipment—especially given the current environment of increased recycling and reusing—are highly crucial [453–458].

OLEDs' broad and uneven emission spectrum is a complex problem that must be addressed. The light emitted by conventional monochromic OLEDs has a large full width at half maximum (FWHM), usually greater than 60. A broad emission spectrum may help produce a high CRI but also reduces lighting systems' color saturation. Due to their broad emission spectrum, OLED displays rarely match the National Television Standards Committee's (NTSC) color criteria. In this regard, color purity poses a significant barrier to applying OLED technology in premium displays. Furthermore, its complicated device structure, increased power consumption, low-resolution pixel patterning, and limited stability impede its widespread application in commercial devices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum dot nanomaterials of current uses and applications
ce:section.ce:para: As nanoparticles with a distinct track record full of high expectations, dramatic pitfalls, and frequently contentious experimental evidence, quantum dots (QDs) rightfully occupy a particular niche within a steadily growing database of diverse nanomaterials reported as suitable for applications in Nanomedicine [460–468]. One can categorize inorganic nanomaterials according to their source: semiconductors, actinoids, lanthanoids, metal oxides, non-metals, or quantum dots.

However, since inorganic nanomaterials may be bio-functionalized to make them biocompatible, they have become increasingly critical in biomedicine. They can also be made multifunctional by combining broad categories of bio-medically essential tasks, such as tissue engineering, biosensing, therapy, diagnostics, and combinations of these functions like theragnostic [469–471]. Quantum dots contain various nanomaterials, some listed in Table 5 .

It's crucial to remember that the application's needs, such as required optical qualities, toxicity concerns, and compatibility with current technologies, determine the best choice of nanomaterial. Table 5 lists the types of nanomaterials utilized in quantum dots, their definitions, and a summary of their applications and uses. To further improve the performance and adaptability of quantum dots in numerous sectors, ongoing research is concentrated on creating new nanomaterials and refining existing characteristics.

GQDs possess tunable optical properties and unique characteristics, including a high specific surface area, resistance to photobleaching, low cytotoxicity, and excellent biocompatibility. These features and enhanced cell penetration capability make GQDs a versatile biosensing platform and promising bioimaging probes [500]. QD-LEDs are used in high-resolution displays to get accurate and beautiful colors. They are also utilized in energy-efficient lighting systems and solar cells to improve energy conversion efficiency [501]. Quantum dots are fluorescent markers in biological applications that enable accurate imaging and diagnosis. Furthermore, they are. These applications demonstrate the wide range of uses for quantum dots in electronics, energy from renewable sources, and healthcare [502].

Since quantum dots (QDs) can be tailored to specific applications like nonlinear optical devices (NLO), electro-optical devices, and computing applications, they represent a promising zero-dimensional advanced material. QDs are also referred to as semiconducting nanoparticles [503]. Low toxicity, environmental friendliness, low cost, photostability, favorable charge transfers with enhanced electronic conductivity, and similar ease of synthesis techniques are all desirable attributes of QDs [504]. However, Antibody binding renders QDs ineffective. QD capping materials may elicit complex and dangerous immunological responses. Toxic to the host could be heavy metals found in the core [505]. Regarding quantum yield, resistance to photobleaching, and emission and absorption bandwidths, QDs' optical characteristics significantly outperform those of organic dyes. Additionally, QDs can be loaded with pharmaceuticals or targeting moieties, making them viable options for drug administration, such as in the treatment of cancer and site-specific medical imaging. Researchers showed earlier this year that although QDs accumulated in lymph nodes, bone marrow, liver, and spleen for up to three months following injection, QD-based imaging on monkeys had no adverse side effects. However, the fact that QDs remain persistent in living animals raises questions regarding the safety of employing QDs in clinical and laboratory settings [506]. The most prevalent varieties of nanomaterials utilized in quantum dots are shown in Fig. 14
.

Quantum dot luminophores in light-emitting diodes hold the potential to create next-generation displays [507]. However, the unique chemical, optical, and optoelectronic features of QDs and their mature synthetic chemistry have contributed significantly to their quick success in the last five years for use in displays. These developments are not coincidental [508]. On the other hand, Colloidal quantum dots (QDs) have several unique properties, including solid photostability, easy color tenability, pure color emission with very narrow bandwidths, and high luminescence efficiency [509]. Moreover, the use of quantum dot nanoparticles in display technologies is covered in detail in this section. The article highlights the significant improvement in color accuracy, brightness, and energy efficiency that quantum dots can bring about in LEDs and screens. There are several methods for quantum Dot patterning techniques, which are shown in Fig. 15 below.

These approaches achieve nanoscale precision in the arrangement and positioning of quantum dots by employing techniques like molecular beam epitaxy, electron beam lithography, and dip-pen nanolithography. The produced patterns make it easier to create sophisticated optoelectronic devices and investigate quantum dot capabilities and interactions at the nanoscale.

Table 6 discusses the comparative analysis of the display styles, including their performance and difficulties. QDs (CdSe) displays provide better color and energy efficiency. PQD displays have a high level of stability and brightness. Vibrant colors are available on bending screens with QD-based flexible displays.

Using hot photo-generated carriers to produce greater photo voltages or higher photocurrents, quantum dot (QD) solar cells can raise the highest achievable thermodynamic conversion efficiency of solar photon conversion to around 66 %. The former effect in QD array photo electrodes is based on mini band transport and hot carrier collection before their relaxation at the band boundaries through phonon emission [511]. On the other hand, recent colloidal quantum dot research has resulted in significant advancements in synthesis processes, material film processing techniques, optoelectronic property assessment, and optimization [512]. High-efficiency solar cells result from the recent boom in using semiconductor nanostructures for solar energy conversion [513]. Mainly, Devices for photovoltaic solutions gather much solar energy to convert into electricity while keeping production costs lower than those of traditional crystalline semiconductor devices [514]. Additionally, a new concept arises for Semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), which are incredibly intriguing materials for the construction of solar cells; yet, at the moment, the most effective cells have been made with hazardous heavy metals like Pb or Cd. With no trace of Cd or Pb, solar cells built using "green" QDs have a moderate efficiency of 2.52 % [515].

There are various benefits to quantum dot solar cells, which are shown in Fig. 16 . To fully realize the potential of quantum dot solar cells as a practical clean energy technology, taking advantage of the benefits of quantum dot materials is imperative.

Quantum dots (QDs) are colloidal nanoparticles with exceptional optical and electronic characteristics brought about by quantum confinement processes. Because of their superior optical qualities, QDs are perfect for fluorescence imaging of biological systems [516]. The nanoparticles also greatly help bio-imaging applications by mitigating the extreme toxicity that inorganic materials generally bring. Without compromising functionality, the smaller size can allow for higher sensitivity and a lower effective dose [517]. As early as the 1980s, groundbreaking research on the synthesis of quantum dots was presented. Generally, Bawendi et al. did exquisite work in 1993 to create organometallic synthetic techniques for the organic phase production of extremely luminous QDs. In their work, they used dimethyl cadmium as the cadmium precursor to generate CdSe nanocrystals with a monodispersed size distribution and good crystallinity [518]. Their high luminescence efficiency, low toxicity, biocompatibility, and biological inertness have been emphasized as biological markers, live imaging, immunosensors, carriers, and drug-delivering agents [519]. In an article, they found that to showcase the unparalleled amalgamation of profound penetration, elevated spatial resolution, multicolored imaging, and rapid acquisition velocity provided by the SWIR quantum dots, we simultaneously and real-time measured the lipoprotein metabolic turnover rates in multiple organs in mice, along with the heartbeat and breathing rates of conscious and unconstrained creatures. Additionally, we produced meticulous three-dimensional quantitative flow maps of the mouse brain vasculature [520]. Also, in another article, the synthesis of biocompatible FeSe QDs was accomplished with success, and at high QD concentrations (up to 70 μg ml−1), good cell survival was noted. Two- and three-photon fluorescence imaging was performed using the QDs [521].

Fig. 17 shows quantum dots' distinct optical characteristics and adaptable functions, which make them valuable instruments for biomedical imaging applications. These include improved sensitivity, multiplexing capacities, and compatibility with cutting-edge imaging methods for scientific and clinical diagnoses.

Simple fluorescent tags, like those used in immunoassays, to intrinsic sensors that use QDs' innate photo-physical reaction to changes in temperature, electric field, or ion concentration are just a few of the applications for QDs in sensors. A third component is added to QDs and bimolecular recognition moieties like antibodies to modify the optical signal through energy transfer in more intricate setups. In energy transfer-based sensors employing charge or electron transfer, nanometer surface energy transfer (NSET), or Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET), QDs can function as donors, acceptors, or both [522,523]. The number of biosensors that use CQDs or GQDs reported thus far is small, but it is anticipated to proliferate [524]. Field-effect transistors, photovoltaics, light-emitting diodes, electrochemical sensors, glucose sensors, PL sensors, ECL sensors, bioimaging, and bio-labelling have all been studied in the past about GQDs [525,526]. A glucose sensor is an all-purpose system for natural glucose detection that uses a cationic boronic corrosive substituted bipyridinium (BBV) salt and several fluorescent GQDs with polar surface assemblies of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. As seen in Fig. 18 [527], BBV served as a glucose receptor and a fluorescence quencher in this sensor, whereas GQDs performed as a fluorescent component.

In recent years, there has been notable advancement in modifying the characteristics of quantum dots (QDs) that do not include cadmium (non-Cd QDs) for lighting applications. The objectives encompass the creation of new materials, enhancement of quantum efficiency and thermal stability, and, crucially, the ability to manufacture them on a sufficiently large scale to satisfy the requirements of the lighting sector. The features of the three most mature non-Cd quantum dot material systems, namely InP, CuInS2, and doped ZnS/ZnSe, will be discussed about their suitability for lighting applications. Among these three systems, the InP-based quantum dots (QDs) with a core/shell structure are now accessible with a quantum efficiency (QE) equivalent to the highest-performing CdSe QDs. Additionally, they are available in sufficient quantities to fulfill lighting requirements. The CuInS2 quantum dots (QDs) have also emerged as a non-toxic alternative to CdSe QDs. However, they require substantial improvement to reach a comparable level of performance as InP and CdSe QDs [528]. Commercial items are required to adhere to safety standards and comply with legislation. QDs produced from the III-V group elements (InP), from the I-III-VI2 group (CuInS2), and Mn2+ doped ZnS and ZnSe nanocrystals have gained significant attention due to this reason. Recently, they have achieved a performance level equivalent to QDs based on Cd. These alternatives are appealing substitutes for Cd-based quantum dots because they exhibit non-toxic properties. These QDs have been shown to have practical uses in converting light and in creating electroluminescent (EL) devices [529,530]. Non-toxic quantum dots (QDs) can potentially replace rare-earth-activated inorganic phosphors in lighting applications. In the long run, this might lessen our reliance on rare-earth metals, which are regarded as crucial due to their limited availability and high cost. This text discusses the latest advancements in the creation of non-Cadmium Quantum Dots (QDs) and their potential use in lighting applications shortly. The acceptability of luminous quantum dots (QDs) in solid-state lighting (SSL) technology depends on several crucial factors, including color tunability, purity, quantum efficiency, quick radiative decay, photostability, thermal performance, and the ability to scale up the synthesis procedure. Currently, the most favorable materials are InP-based (QDs), CuInS2 quantum dots, and doped zinc silver and/or Zinc selenium QDs [528].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Future directions for quantum dot nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: Nanohybrid materials based on quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted much interest in various research applications, especially in the biomedical sectors, because of their exceptional biocompatibility and distinct physicochemical features compared to other nanomaterials [531]. One of the efficient ways to produce graphene-based fluorescent materials is to transform one-dimensional (1D) graphene into zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots (GQDs). These GQDs provide an emerging nanolight with outstanding features because of their exceptional quantum confinement and edge effects. The most recent research on GQDs is provided in this review. A summary of the synthetic methods was provided, focusing on the top-down approaches, which have the advantages of plentiful raw materials, large-scale production, and ease of use [532]. Scientists are fascinated and drawn to manufactured nanoscale products like quantum dots (QDs) because of their potential for simultaneous imaging and targeting in drug delivery for pharmaceutical and therapeutic purposes [533].

Fig. 19 Talks about the current research being done to improve the performance and stability of quantum dot solar cells by addressing the issues they confront. Examine how quantum dot solar cells might be used in areas other than typical photovoltaics, like wearable technology, portable power sources, and building-integrated photovoltaics (BIPV). Quantum computing and quantum communication topics will be the most demanding in the future.

The photoluminescence (PL) features of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) remain unsatisfactory, despite the proposal of potential reasons such as size effect, surface modification, and doping with additional substances. Although GQDs with various colors of photoluminescence (PL), including PL in the near-infrared (NIR) region, have been achieved, the quantum concentrations of most GQDs remain low. Therefore, it is crucial to enhance the performance of GQDs due to their limited utility in immunosensing caused by their low quantum yields. The potential of metal-enhanced fluorescence to increase quantum yield has been demonstrated in a recent study. Despite the exciting and encouraging achievements, utilizing GQDs for immunosensing applications is still in its early stages, with numerous hurdles yet to be overcome. There is a need to create a novel surface modification approach for use in immunosensing applications. Due to their consistent size, exceptional photoluminescence, and elevated quantum yields, GQDs will undoubtedly be employed in a broader range of innovative applications. To summarise, GQDs are a new type of nanomaterial that can be used for immunosensing. To effectively utilize GQDs, fostering collaboration between many disciplines, such as chemistry, biological sciences, physics, and medicine, is important [534].

Quantum dot nanomaterials have become a revolutionary technology in multiple domains, such as solar energy conversion, bioimaging, and optoelectronic devices, because of their distinctive optical and electrical characteristics resulting from quantum confinement. Due to their high quantum yield and light emission that depends on their size, these materials are extremely advantageous for applications that demand accurate manipulation of light and energy. However, there are still notable obstacles to overcome, like the considerable expenses associated with production, the potential for toxicity, and the environmental instability. Conventional synthesis processes, although efficient, can require costly and dangerous substances, which restrict the scalability and safety of quantum dot applications.

Novel synthesis methods are being developed to overcome these limits, providing safer, more economical, and environmentally sustainable alternatives. Nonorganometallic techniques and microwave-assisted methods have demonstrated potential in improving the resistance to light-induced degradation and decreasing the harmfulness of quantum dots. This review focuses on the progress made in synthesis techniques and the diverse range of applications of quantum dots, showcasing its capacity to transform the field of nanotechnology. Current research addresses current obstacles and broadens the range of sectors in which quantum dots can be used. As these technologies progress, quantum dots are positioned to have a growing and essential role in the next generation of high-performing, energy-efficient materials that are compatible with living organisms.
This research did not receive any funding.
